---
title: "ps2_Zeyu"
author: "Zeyu(Zey)"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

## PS 2

### 3.1

(a) What is a standard error? What is the difference between a standard error and a standard deviation?

Standard error is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. It measures how much the estimated statistic is expected to vary across repeated random samples.

Standard deviation measures the variability of individual observations within a sample or population.

(b) How is randomization inference used to test the sharp null hypothesis of no effect for any subject?

1.  First, assume the sharp null is true.

2.  Calculate a test statistic (e.g., the difference in means) for the observed assignment.

3.  Generate the distribution of the test statistic under all possible (or many randomly sampled) treatment assignments.

4.  Compare the observed statistic with this randomization distribution to get the p-value. If the observed statistic is very unlikely under the randomization distribution, we reject the sharp null hypothesis.

(c) What is a 95% confidence interval?

A 95% confidence interval is an interval constructed from sample data using a procedure that, over repeated random samples, would contain the true population parameter 95% of the time.

(d) How does complete random assignment differ from block random assignment and cluster random assignment?

Complete random assignment randomly assigns individual subjects to treatment or control without considering any subject characteristics.

Block random assignment first groups subjects into homogeneous blocks based on characteristics (e.g., gender or age), and then randomly assigns treatment within each block.

Cluster random assignment randomly assigns entire groups, such as schools or villages, rather than individuals.

(e) Experiments that assign the same number of subjects to the treatment group and control group are said to have a "balanced design." What are some desirable statistical properties of balanced designs?

Maximize statistical power for a given total sample size.
Minimize the variance (or standard error) of the estimated treatment effect.
Produce more precise estimates and narrower confidence intervals.

### 3.5

(a) Calculate the estimated difference-in-means for all seven possible randomizations.

| Treated village | (Y(1)) | (Y(0)) | Treatment effect | Control mean | Difference in means |
|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | 15 | 10 | 5 | 95/6=15.833 | −0.833 |
| 2 | 15 | 15 | 0 | 90/6=15 | 0 |
| 3 | 30 | 20 | 10 | 85/6=14.167 | 15.833 |
| 4 | 15 | 20 | -5 | 85/6=14.167 | 0.833 |
| 5 | 20 | 10 | 10 | 95/6=15.833 | 4.167 |
| 6 | 15 | 15 | 0 | 90/6=15 | 0 |
| 7 | 30 | 15 | 15 | 90/6=15 | 15 |

(b) Show that the average of these estimates is the true ATE.

$$ATE=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{n}​(Yi​(1)−Yi​(0))=\frac{5+0+10-5+10+0+15}{7}=5$$

While mean of difference-in-means is:

$$\frac{-0.833+0+15.833+0.833+4.167+0+15}{7}=5$$
